title: medical
instructions:
I tell myself to slow down, but my body won’t listen.
 Why is my heart beating so loudly?
 Why does the monitor sound like it’s speeding up every time I look at it?
 I squeeze my eyes shut, hoping the numbers will settle if I stop staring. They don’t.
 Someone walks past my door quickly. A cart rattles.
 I flinch.
 Was that for me? Did something happen? Are they coming in because they saw something drop on the monitor?
 A dull ache spreads down my arm from the IV site.
 I try to adjust my position, but the crinkly sheet sticks to my skin.
 My palms feel damp. I can’t tell if it’s sweat or if I’m just imagining it.
 The door opens. A nurse steps in with a tablet.
 I watch her expression so carefully it feels like I’m dissecting every micro‑movement.
 Is that concern? Is she trying not to alarm me?
 Just tell me everything’s fine. Please just say it’s routine.
 She doesn’t. She just says the doctor will be in soon.
 My throat tightens.
 Soon? Why soon? Why not now? What did the tests show? Why isn’t anyone telling me anything?
 The monitor beeps unevenly for a moment.
 My breath catches.
 There. That. That wasn’t normal. I knew it. Something is wrong. They’re not saying it, but something is wrong.
 My legs tremble under the blanket.
 I keep trying to stretch my toes to stop the shaking, but that only makes me notice it more.
 A sudden wave of heat climbs up my face.
 Calm down.
 Just calm down.
 But saying it only makes my chest squeeze tighter, like the room is leaning in closer.
 The nurse adjusts a cable on the monitor.
 I watch her hands—steady, efficient, focused.
 I wait for her to look at the screen afterward, to give me some kind of signal.

It’s early evening inside a cramped command post set up across from an office building.
 You pull your shoulders tight as you lean over the radio, headset pressing against your temples.
 Your eyes track the windows on the third floor, searching for any shift in movement.
 A sudden thud echoes from inside the building.
 Your pulse jumps. The noise reverberates through the room, followed by a tense silence that makes your ears buzz.
 Your breathing grows shallow. You see the tactical team freeze in place.
 You clench your jaw and steady your grip on the microphone. A colleague whispers that the suspect has stopped responding.
 You think: “I can’t lose control of this conversation.” Heat creeps up the back of your neck.
 You narrow your focus, trying to catch any flicker of motion behind the blinds, but the glass reflects only the fading light.
 Now you have to reestablish contact before the situation worsens.
 Your hands tremble slightly as you adjust the volume. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to project calm even though the negotiator beside you is signaling that the timeline is shrinking.
 Your stomach tightens. Your back stiffens. Multiple voices talk over each other behind you, none offering a clear plan.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in. Suddenly you realize you haven’t checked the secondary phone line.
 You scan the table, searching for the blinking indicator you should have noticed earlier.
 Your heart thuds against your ribs.
 Your fingers slip slightly on the radio switch as you try to steady your hand. Sweat gathers at your hairline.
 Your throat feels dry. You feel stretched thin in the thick tension of the room.
 You think about the people inside, hoping someone is still listening.
 You inhale the stale air of the temporary command center.
 You feel drained, hollow, and unsteady. Your eyes sting as you blink hard, trying to stay focused.

title: Natural disaster evacuation
instructions:
It’s just before dawn at a crowded evacuation center on the edge of a coastal town.
 You pull your shoulders tight as you stand in the humid air, the wind pushing grit against your face.
 Your eyes sweep the parking lot, trying to track the incoming buses through the swirling debris.
 A deep rumble rolls across the ground.
 Your pulse jumps. The vibration climbs up your legs, followed by a sharp crack from somewhere behind the shelter.
 Your breathing turns shallow. You see a utility pole sway in the distance.
 You clench your jaw and steady your grip on the clipboard. A volunteer rushes over, saying the route you planned is now blocked.
 You think: “We’re losing time we don’t have.” Heat gathers at the back of your neck.
 You narrow your focus, trying to spot the last families you were supposed to guide out, but the crowd shifts unpredictably.
 Now you have to redirect everyone before the storm surge reaches the highway.
 Your hands tremble slightly as you wave people forward. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to stay calm even though the loudspeakers keep cutting out.
 Your stomach tightens. Your back stiffens. Voices overlap around you, each one asking for instructions you don’t have.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in. Suddenly you realize you haven’t checked the far exit.
 You scan the edge of the lot, trying to see if the gate is still open.
 Your heart thuds against your ribs.
 Your fingers slip slightly on your radio as you try to call for an update. Sweat gathers at your hairline.
 Your throat feels dry. You feel stretched thin in the rising wind.
 You think about the people still waiting inside, hoping someone will tell them where to go.
 You inhale the sharp scent of wet concrete and diesel.
 You feel drained, hollow, and unsteady. Your eyes sting as you blink hard, trying to stay focused.

title: Mountain Climbing accident
instructions:
A sudden crack echoes across the slope.
 Your pulse spikes. The sound vibrates through your chest, followed by a low rumble that makes the air feel thinner.
 Your breathing stutters. You see loose powder sliding down the incline.
 You clench your teeth and tighten your grip on the rope. A frayed section catches your eye, and panic crawls up your spine.
 You think: I can’t lose them up here.Heat rises beneath your layers despite the freezing air.
 You lean forward, searching for any movement below, but the storm blurs everything into shifting white.
 Now you have to decide whether to descend or hold your position as the wind grows harsher.
 Your hands tremble as you test the anchor. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to stay steady even though the ridge groans under the weight of fresh snow.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. The radio crackles with half-formed words you can’t make out.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by the cliff behind you and the drop ahead. Suddenly you realize you’ve lost track of your footing.
 You shift your weight, trying to find solid rock beneath your crampons.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your gloves slip slightly on the carabiner as you try to secure the line. Sweat chills instantly on your forehead.
 Your throat tightens. You feel stretched thin in the roar of the wind.
 You think about the person depending on you, hoping you haven’t already run out of time.
 You inhale the sharp scent of ice and metal.
 You feel the moment tightening around you, every instinct urging you to move before the storm closes in.
 Your vision blurs at the edges as you force yourself to stay present, refusing to let fear take the final word.

title: House Fire
instructions:
Your eyes sweep the shifting haze, trying to locate the source of the shouting you heard moments ago.
 A sudden crash erupts somewhere deeper inside.
 Your pulse surges. The noise rattles through your chest, followed by a wave of sparks drifting across the ceiling.
 Your breathing turns uneven. You see the glow intensify along the far wall.
 You clench your jaw and tighten your grip on the hose. A teammate calls out that the interior layout isn’t matching the map.
 You think: “We’re losing precious seconds.” Warmth builds beneath your gear despite the suffocating smoke.
 You lean forward, trying to make out any movement through the swirling orange light, but the air warps everything.
 Now you have to push farther in before the structure weakens.
 Your hands tremble as you advance. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to stay steady even though the floor vibrates under your boots.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. The radio spits out fragmented updates you can’t fully hear.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by collapsing debris and thick smoke. Suddenly you realize you’ve lost sight of the exit path.
 You pivot slowly, searching for the reflective markers you should have seen.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your gloves slip slightly on the nozzle as you adjust your stance. Sweat stings your eyes beneath the mask.
 Your throat tightens. You feel stretched thin in the roar of the flames.
 You think about the person who might still be inside, hoping you’re not already too late.
 You inhale the sharp scent of burning insulation and hot metal.
 You steady your breath as a faint silhouette flickers through the haze, a reminder that you’re not done yet.
 You push forward with renewed focus, trusting that every step you take is one closer to pulling someone back into the light.

title: Plane emergency landing
instructions:
A sharp metallic groan vibrates through the floor.
 Your pulse leaps. The sound climbs up your spine, followed by a sudden drop that makes your ears ring.
 Your breathing turns uneven. You see overhead bins trembling with each shudder.
 You clench your jaw and brace your feet against the seat in front of you. A flight attendant leans in, voice tight, telling everyone to prepare.
 You think: “This can’t be happening right now.” Heat spreads across your face despite the cold air blasting from the vents.
 You lean forward, trying to catch a glimpse out the window, but the clouds smear everything into a gray blur.
 Now you have to follow instructions you can barely hear over the engine noise.
 Your hands tremble as you tighten the belt. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to stay calm even though the plane keeps dipping unpredictably.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. The intercom crackles with broken phrases you can’t piece together.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by rows of seats and panicked breathing. Suddenly you realize you don’t know where the nearest exit actually is.
 You twist in your seat, scanning for the glowing signs you should have noticed earlier.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your palms slip slightly on the armrests as you try to steady yourself. Sweat gathers at your hairline.
 Your throat tightens. You feel stretched thin in the roar of the engines.
 You think about the people waiting for you on the ground, hoping this landing goes right.
 You draw a slow breath as the cabin steadies for a moment, a fragile pause that feels like the sky holding its breath with you.
 You brace for whatever comes next, clinging to the hope that the wheels will find the runway and carry you safely back to solid ground.

title: Shipwreck survival
instructions:
Cold spray lashes your face as you cling to the splintered edge of an overturned lifeboat.
 Your shoulders lock while you fight to keep your balance against the heaving swells.
 Your eyes sweep the dark horizon, trying to spot any sign of lights through the sheets of rain.
 A deep boom rolls beneath the water.
 Your pulse surges. The vibration climbs up your arms, followed by a sudden tilt that nearly pulls you under.
 Your breathing stutters. You see debris rising and falling with each violent wave.
 You clench your jaw and tighten your grip on the rope. Someone shouts your name, their voice nearly swallowed by the wind.
 You think: “We can’t stay out here much longer.” Heat and cold mix strangely across your skin.
 You lean forward, trying to make out shapes in the storm, but everything blurs into shifting shadows.
 Now you have to decide whether to paddle toward the faint glow or stay put in the chaos.
 Your hands tremble as you adjust your hold. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to stay steady even though the boat keeps lurching unpredictably.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. The emergency beacon crackles with static instead of a signal.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by towering waves and endless darkness. Suddenly you realize you’ve drifted far from the others.
 You twist around, searching for silhouettes you should still be able to see.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your fingers slip slightly on the soaked rope as you try to pull yourself upright. Saltwater stings your eyes.
 Your throat tightens. You feel stretched thin in the roar of the storm.
 You think about the people waiting for you on shore, hoping rescue is already on the way.

title: cave rescue
instructions:
Cold mist clings to the cavern walls as headlamps sweep across uneven stone, each beam catching dust suspended in the still air.
 Boots scrape against the narrow ledge while rescuers inch forward, voices low to keep the echoes from masking distant sounds.
 Eyes scan every crevice, searching for the faintest movement in the darkness ahead.
 A muffled call bounces through the tunnels.
 Heartbeats spike as the sound ripples through the rock, followed by a tense silence that sharpens every breath.
 Breathing turns shallow; the air feels colder the deeper the team moves.
 Someone adjusts their harness with trembling hands, the metal clips chiming softly.
 A single thought cuts through the tension: “We’re close, but the passage is tightening.”
 The ceiling dips lower as the path narrows into a tight squeeze.
 Decision-making becomes urgent as they debate whether to push forward or reroute.
 Gloves brush against damp stone, fingertips numb from the chill.
 Instructions crackle through the radio, distorted by the twisting tunnels.
 A heavy pressure settles in each chest as the cave shifts with a low groan.
 The space feels smaller by the moment, boxed in by jagged walls and the weight of the mountain.
 A realization hits hard—one wrong move could block the only exit.
 The team pauses, listening for another sign of life.
 Heartbeats pound like drumbeats in the confined dark.
 Sweat gathers beneath helmets despite the cold, palms slipping on rope.
 Throats tighten as another faint cry echoes from deeper inside.
 Thoughts race toward the trapped climber waiting in the dark.
 The scent of wet earth and mineral dust fills every breath.
 A hollow, trembling feeling spreads through each rescuer as they press on, focus the only thing keeping panic at bay.

title: Child Gone Missing
instructions:
It’s late afternoon under harsh hospital lights.
 I’m lying on a stiff bed with my chest rising too fast, the monitor beeping quicker every time I glance at it.
 Footsteps rush past the door and a cart rattles, making me flinch as I wonder if something changed on the screen.
 A nurse enters with a tablet, expression unreadable, saying only that the doctor will be in soon.
 My throat tightens as the monitor stutters and my palms dampen against the rails.
 Two staff members arrive speaking in clipped numbers before telling me they need to repeat a test.
 They leave and the room feels too big, my pulse thudding in my neck as the monitor gives a long, uneven beep.
 The lights blur overhead and my eyes sting as I blink, trying to steady myself against the rising pressure in my thoughts.
 I grip the bed rails, wishing someone would explain what’s happening, wishing the silence didn’t feel so heavy.
 The ceiling swims slightly as the brightness sharpens, every hallway sound landing like it’s meant for me.
 My breath catches as the monitor hiccups again, a tiny blip that sends my nerves spiraling.
 I try to swallow the tightness in my throat, but it lingers, warm and unsteady.
 The room feels too bright, too still, like it’s waiting for something I’m not prepared to hear.
 My legs tremble under the blanket as I shift, trying to ground myself against the crinkling sheets.
 A wave of heat rises across my face, and I close my eyes, hoping the feeling will pass.
 The hum of machines fills the space, steady but somehow overwhelming, like they’re all listening with me.
 I focus on the rhythm of my breath, but it keeps slipping, shallow and uneven.
 I blink up at the ceiling again, the lights blurring into soft halos.
 All I want is for someone to walk in and tell me what’s going on before my thoughts turn every pause into a warning I can’t ignore.

title: Car Crash
instructions:
Night air presses against the cracked windshield as you sit stranded on a deserted stretch of highway.
 Your shoulders tense while you steady yourself, the steering wheel still vibrating faintly beneath your hands.
 Your eyes sweep the empty road, trying to make sense of the twisted shadows cast by your flickering headlights.
 A distant echo rolls through the hills.
 Your pulse jumps. The sound bounces off the rocks, followed by a silence that feels too sharp.
 Your breathing turns uneven. You see dust drifting slowly across the beam of light.
 You clench your jaw and reach for your phone, only to see the signal icon vanish.
 You think: “No one even knows I’m out here.” Heat rises beneath your collar despite the cold night.
 You lean forward, trying to spot any passing car, but the darkness swallows the horizon.
 Now you have to decide whether to stay put or step out into the wind.
 Your hands tremble as you push the door open. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to stay calm even though the engine keeps making a low, unsettling click.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. The quiet feels too heavy, too absolute.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by the cliffs on one side and the drop-off on the other. Suddenly you realize you can’t remember which direction you came from.
 You turn slowly, scanning for tire tracks you should still be able to see.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your fingers slip slightly on the cold metal as you steady yourself against the hood. Sweat chills instantly on your forehead.
 Your throat tightens. You feel stretched thin in the vast emptiness around you.
 You think about the people waiting for you, hoping you’re already on your way home.
 You inhale the sharp scent of dust and overheated rubber.
 You feel drained, hollow, and unsteady. Your eyes sting as you blink hard, trying to stay focused.

title: Industrial Accident
instructions:
A sharp hiss cuts through the noise.
 Your pulse spikes. The sound slices through the air, followed by a vibration that rattles the metal railing under your hand.
 Your breathing turns shallow. You see steam curling upward in frantic bursts.
 You clamp your jaw and tighten your grip on the emergency shutoff lever, only to realize it’s jammed.
 You think: “If this escalates, we’re in real trouble.” Heat gathers at the back of your neck.
 You pivot toward the catwalk, hoping to spot a supervisor, but the haze makes every silhouette look distorted.
 Now you have to decide whether to climb toward the control room or stay and try to force the valve.
 Your hands tremble as you brace your boots against the grated floor. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to stay focused even though the machinery keeps shuddering like it’s about to fail.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. The intercom spits out garbled instructions you can’t decipher.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by towering equipment and narrow walkways. Suddenly you realize you haven’t checked the pressure release panel.
 You turn sharply, scanning for the blinking indicator you should have noticed earlier.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your gloves slip slightly on the slick metal as you try to steady yourself. Sweat gathers beneath your helmet.
 Your throat tightens. You feel stretched thin in the roar of machinery and alarms.
 You think about the workers on the lower level, hoping they’ve already moved to safety.
 You inhale the sharp scent of hot oil and scorched metal.
 You feel drained, hollow, and unsteady. Your eyes sting as you blink hard, trying to stay focused.

title: Violent Home Intrusion
instructions:
Your eyes sweep the dim space, searching for anything that explains why the lamp you turned off earlier is now glowing faintly.
 A soft thud breaks the silence.
 Your pulse surges. The noise ripples through your chest, followed by a stillness that feels unnatural.
 Your breathing turns shallow. You notice the curtain near the back door swaying even though the air is still.
 You clamp your jaw and inch forward, every step feeling too loud on the hardwood floor.
 You think: “Someone shouldn’t be here.” Heat gathers at the base of your neck.
 You lean just far enough to see the edge of the living room, but the shadows distort everything into unfamiliar shapes.
 Now you have to decide whether to retreat or confront whatever caused the disturbance.
 Your hands tremble as you reach for your phone, only to realize the battery is nearly dead.
 You’re supposed to stay calm even though the house feels smaller with every passing second.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. A faint creak from upstairs makes your heart lurch.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by the narrow hallway and the darkened rooms. Suddenly you realize you left the front door unlocked.
 You turn slowly, scanning for any movement you might have missed.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your fingers slip slightly on the doorknob as you test it, trying not to make a sound. Sweat gathers at your hairline.
 Your throat tightens. You feel stretched thin in the thick, suffocating quiet.
 You think about the people who live here with you, hoping they’re safe wherever they are.
 You inhale the faint scent of dust and cold night air drifting through the entryway.
 You feel drained, hollow, and unsteady. Your eyes sting as you blink hard, trying to stay focused.

title: Sudden Cariac Arrest
instructions:
The moment the conversation stops mid‑sentence, a strange stillness settles over the room.
 Your breath catches as you turn toward them, something in their posture instantly wrong.
 Your eyes widen, trying to make sense of the sudden slackness in their expression.
 A chair scrapes sharply against the floor.
 Your pulse surges. The sound feels too loud in the quiet, followed by a rush of air as you stumble forward.
 Your breathing turns shallow. You see their chest unmoving, their hands limp at their sides.
 You clamp your jaw and drop to your knees, your fingers trembling as you reach for their shoulder.
 You think: “This can’t be happening — not now.” Heat floods your face despite the cold shock running through you.
 You lean closer, listening for anything — a breath, a sound, a sign — but the silence presses in.
 Now you have to act even though your mind is spinning.
 Your hands shake as you fumble for your phone, nearly dropping it as you try to dial.
 You’re supposed to stay calm even though your thoughts are racing faster than you can control.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. The seconds feel stretched and distorted.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by the walls, the furniture, the weight of the moment. Suddenly you realize you can’t remember the exact steps you practiced.
 You force yourself to focus, scanning their face for any sign of movement.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your palms slip slightly as you position your hands, sweat gathering despite the cold fear tightening your throat.
 You feel stretched thin, caught between urgency and disbelief.
 You think about the last thing they said, hoping help arrives in time.

title: Wildifre Approaching a town
instructions:
Ash drifts across the main street as sirens wail in the distance, the sky turning an unnatural shade of orange.
Heat presses down like a weight while residents rush past, clutching bags and pets and whatever they could grab.
Eyes dart toward the ridgeline where a wall of smoke churns upward, swallowing the horizon.
A low roar rolls through the valley.
Heartbeats spike as the sound deepens, a reminder that the fire is moving faster than anyone expected.
Breaths come shallow; the air tastes metallic and dry.
Someone shouts for everyone to head toward the evacuation point, their voice cracking with urgency.
A single thought cuts through the noise: “There isn’t much time left.”
Wind shifts suddenly, sending sparks spiraling over rooftops.
Decision-making becomes frantic as roads clog and visibility drops.
Hands shake while fumbling with car keys, the heat intensifying even before the flames are visible.
Instructions blare from loudspeakers, but the words blur together.
A tightness settles in the chest as the smoke thickens.
The town feels smaller by the second, boxed in by hills and the advancing glow.
A realization hits hard—one of the escape routes might already be compromised.
People turn in circles, scanning for any open path.
Heartbeats pound like drumbeats in the ears.
Sweat gathers despite the dry air, palms slipping on door handles and steering wheels.
Throats tighten as the wind carries the crackle of burning brush.
Thoughts race toward the people not yet accounted for.
The scent of scorched pine and dust fills every breath.
A hollow, drained feeling spreads through the body as the sky darkens, and focus becomes the only thing holding panic at bay.

title: Bridge Collapse
instructions:
A deep shudder ripples through the roadway as traffic slows to a crawl, the guardrails vibrating in a way that feels wrong.
Drivers lean forward in their seats, confused, while a low groan rises from somewhere beneath the asphalt.
The sky is calm, but the bridge trembles again, harder this time, sending a jolt through every parked bumper.
A sharp crack echoes underfoot.
Heartbeats spike as the sound ricochets between the steel beams, followed by a sudden lurch that tilts the entire span.
Breaths turn shallow; loose gravel skitters across the pavement.
Someone shouts to move, their voice swallowed by the grinding of metal.
A single thought cuts through the noise: “This structure isn’t holding.”
Cars jerk forward in panic, tires screeching as drivers try to escape the swaying deck.
Decision-making becomes frantic as lanes bottleneck and the railing shifts inches at a time.
Hands shake on steering wheels, the vibration growing stronger with every second.
Instructions blare from a distant loudspeaker, but the words distort in the wind.
A tightness settles in the chest as the bridge dips again.
The span feels smaller by the moment, boxed in by stalled vehicles and the yawning drop below.
A realization hits hard—one of the support pillars might already be compromised.
People abandon their cars, turning in circles, searching for any stable path.
Heartbeats pound like hammer blows in the ears.
Sweat gathers despite the cool air, palms slipping on door handles and railings.
Throats tighten as the cables overhead snap taut with a sharp metallic whine.
Thoughts race toward the people still stuck farther back.

title: Chemical Spill Evac
instructions:
A sharp chemical odor drifts across the industrial district as warning sirens erupt without explanation, slicing through the afternoon air.
People step out of storefronts in confusion, glancing toward the billowing plume rising beyond the warehouses.
The sky takes on a strange, pale tint as a low hum vibrates through the ground.
A metallic clang echoes from somewhere down the street.
Heartbeats spike as the sound reverberates between buildings, followed by a sudden announcement ordering immediate evacuation.
Breaths turn shallow; the air feels heavier with every passing second.
Someone shouts for everyone to move uphill, their voice cracking with urgency.
A single thought cuts through the noise: “Whatever leaked is spreading fast.”
Traffic snarls instantly as cars try to funnel into the same narrow road.
Decision-making becomes frantic as people abandon vehicles and start running.
Hands shake while clutching bags, phones, and masks that don’t seem to fit properly.
Instructions blare from loudspeakers, but the words distort into static.
A tightness settles in the chest as the wind shifts, carrying the sharp scent closer.
The neighborhood feels smaller by the moment, boxed in by fences, alleys, and the drifting haze.
A realization hits hard—one of the main exits is already blocked by emergency crews.
Crowds pivot in confusion, searching for any open route.
Heartbeats pound like drumbeats in the ears.
Sweat gathers despite the cool air, palms slipping on car doors and railings.
Throats tighten as the chemical smell intensifies, stinging the eyes.
Thoughts race toward the people still inside nearby buildings.
The scent of solvents and scorched plastic fills every breath.
A hollow, drained feeling spreads through the body as the sirens grow louder, and focus becomes the only thing keeping panic from taking over.

title: Avalanche Survival Gone Missing
instructions:
A distant rumble rolls across the mountainside just as the wind shifts, carrying a sharp burst of powder into the air.
Skiers on the ridge pause mid‑stride, glancing upward as the snowpack trembles in a way that sends a chill through the spine.
The sky is clear, but the slope shivers again, a subtle warning that something massive is shifting above.
A deep crack splits the silence.
Heartbeats spike as the sound echoes through the valley, followed by a sudden roar that swells with terrifying speed.
Breaths turn thin; the cold air feels heavier than before.
Someone yells to move, their voice nearly drowned out by the growing thunder.
A single thought slices through the panic: “The whole face is coming down.”
Snow begins to peel away in sheets, sliding faster than anyone can react.
Decision-making becomes frantic as people scramble toward the treeline.
Hands shake while gripping poles and packs, boots slipping on the unstable crust.
Instructions from a guide ring out, but the words scatter in the wind.
A tightness clamps around the chest as the slope vibrates beneath each step.
The mountainside feels smaller by the moment, boxed in by cliffs and the advancing white wall.
A realization hits hard—one escape route is already swallowed by the slide.
Bodies pivot in desperation, searching for any pocket of safety.
Heartbeats pound like frantic drumbeats in the ears.
Sweat chills instantly on the skin despite the freezing air, palms slipping on icy branches.
Throats tighten as the roar grows louder, shaking the ground.
Thoughts race toward the people farther up the slope who may not have seen the break.
The scent of cold pine and churned snow fills every breath.
A hollow, drained feeling spreads through the body as the avalanche surges closer, and focus becomes the only thing keeping panic from taking over.

title: Kidnapping Escape
instructions:
Footsteps echo down the hallway as the dim room vibrates with the slam of a distant door, the sound jolting every nerve awake.
Shadows stretch across the floor, shifting with each flicker of the lone bulb overhead.
A muffled voice drifts through the walls, too distorted to understand but close enough to spike adrenaline.
A metallic clatter rings out.
Heartbeats surge as the noise reverberates through the cramped space, followed by a silence that feels razor‑thin.
Breaths turn shallow; the air tastes stale and heavy.
Someone outside fumbles with keys, their movements hurried and uneven.
A single thought slices through the panic: “This is the only chance to get out.”
The window latch trembles under your grip, refusing to budge at first.
Decision-making becomes frantic as footsteps return, quicker this time.
Hands shake while tugging at the frame, palms slick with sweat.
Instructions you once heard in a safety class flash through your mind, but the details blur.
A tightness clamps around the chest as the hallway light spills under the door.
The room feels smaller by the moment, boxed in by walls that seem to lean closer.
A realization hits hard—the footsteps have stopped right outside.
Muscles tense as you force the latch one more time, praying it gives.
Heartbeats pound like frantic drumbeats in your ears.
Cold air rushes in as the window finally cracks open, the night outside darker than expected.
Throat tightens while you squeeze through, every movement too loud in the quiet.
Thoughts race toward the people who don’t yet know you’re missing.

title: Offshore Oil Rig Emergency
instructions:
A violent shudder ripples through the platform as the deck lights flicker, casting long, distorted shadows across the steel grating.
Crew members freeze mid‑stride, glancing toward the drilling tower where a deep metallic groan rises from the machinery.
The ocean below churns harder than before, waves slapping the supports with unnerving force.
A sharp alarm pierces the air.
Heartbeats spike as the sound reverberates through the rig, followed by a plume of vapor bursting from a ruptured line.
Breaths turn thin; the air tastes of salt and hot metal.
Someone yells for everyone to move toward muster stations, their voice nearly drowned by the roar of the wind.
A single thought slices through the chaos: “Something critical just failed.”
The catwalk trembles underfoot as workers sprint toward the stairwell.
Decision-making becomes frantic as corridors fill and equipment clatters to the floor.
Hands shake while gripping railings slick with condensation.
Instructions crackle through handheld radios, but the words distort into static.
A tightness clamps around the chest as the rig tilts slightly, just enough to unsettle balance.
The platform feels smaller by the moment, boxed in by pipes, ladders, and the open sea.
A realization hits hard—one of the escape boats might be unreachable.
People pivot in confusion, searching for an alternate route.
Heartbeats pound like frantic drumbeats in the ears.
Sweat gathers despite the cold spray, palms slipping on metal handholds.
Throats tighten as another tremor shakes the structure.

title: Final Story
instructions:
Evening shadows stretch across the park as you move quickly along the winding path.
 Your shoulders tighten while you scan every corner, every bench, every cluster of trees.
 Your eyes sweep the playground, searching for the bright jacket you know so well.
 A distant shout echoes from somewhere near the parking lot.
 Your pulse leaps. The sound vibrates through your chest, followed by a silence that feels too heavy.
 Your breathing turns uneven. You see families gathering their things, none of them the one you’re desperate to find.
 You clench your jaw and grip your phone so hard your fingers ache. A passerby asks if you need help, their voice barely reaching you.
 You think: “I should have been watching more closely.” Heat rises behind your eyes.
 You move faster, trying to retrace the steps from just minutes earlier, but everything looks different in the fading light.
 Now you have to choose which direction to search before darkness settles in.
 Your hands tremble as you call out their name. Your whole body feels wired.
 You’re supposed to stay calm even though the park feels larger with every passing second.
 Your stomach knots. Your back stiffens. Your phone buzzes with messages you can’t bring yourself to read.
 A heavy pressure settles in your chest.
 You feel boxed in by the trees and the growing crowd. Suddenly you realize you haven’t checked the path behind the restrooms.
 You pivot sharply, scanning for any movement you might have missed.
 Your heart hammers against your ribs.
 Your palms slip slightly on the railing as you steady yourself near the steps. Sweat gathers at your hairline despite the cool air.
 Your throat tightens. You feel stretched thin in the rising panic around you.
 You think about the small hand you held earlier, hoping they’re close enough to hear you.
 You inhale the sharp scent of damp leaves and cut grass.
 You feel drained, hollow, and unsteady. Your eyes sting as you blink hard, trying to stay focused.
